Farn Polrrrcar- PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-556,0 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 25,2004
Kathryn Doi
Livingston & Mattesich
l20l K Street, Suite I100
Sacramento, CA 95814-3938
Dear Ms. Doi:
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our File No. 144476
Re
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the City of Benicia
for informal assistance regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act').I
2
3
QUBSTTONS
For the pu{pose of determining whether the'lublic generally''exception applies,
you have asked the following questions:
1. 
If one person owns two pmcels, is the person counted as two "property owners?"
Does a person have to have a certain (percentage) ownership interest in a
property to be counted as a "property ownetr?"
where a parcel is owned by a trus! is the trust considered the "property owner,,
or is each person who has an ownership interest in the tnrst counted as a
property owner?
4. 
May an official count parcels of property rather than property owners?
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the
California Code of Regulations.


File No. I-04-076
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
The Commission counts each person who olvns property as one "property owner.,,
A person who owns multiple properties counts only as one property owner. In
compmison, if two persons (whether individuals or entities) together o\rn one
parcel ofpropert5r, the two persons would count as two "property ownetrs."
A "property ownef is a person who owns real propertyregardles of what
percentage of ownership in the property the person possesses-
Each person with an ownership interest in zuch a trust can be counted as a
property owner for purposes of the "public generall/'exception.
If an official wishes to use parcel data to determine whether the 'public
generally''exception applies, he or she must assess whether theproperty
ownership in his or her jurisdiction is such thx lD/o ofparcels affected equates to
l0o/o or more of all prcperty owners or all hommumers affectod in the official's
jwisdiction or district or 5,000 property owners or homeowners affected in the
official's jrrisdiction. However, the official may not merely "count parcels"
instead of property ovrn€rs (persons).
FACTS
The City of Berricia includes a Downtown Historic Conservation District
("Downtown District'). Three out of five mernbers of the Benicia City Council own
residential or commercial property within the Downtown Dishict orwithin a 500-foot
radius of the Downtown District. The Benicia CommunityDevelopment Departrnent
expects to present to the city council proposed amendments and updates to the downtown
conservation plan to improve land use controls for protwting historic properties. These
proposals, if adopted, may affect the property value of the land within and adjacent to the
Downtown District. The city is attempting to determine ifthe three city council members
may participate in decision-making affecting the Downtown District under the "public
generally'' exception.
The Solano County Assessor's records show a total of llJ62 separately owned
parcels within Benicia's city limits. The Downtown District and the 50G.foot radius
strrounding the Downtown District contain 1,301 sepmately owned parcels.2
The city seeks advice as to whether it is permissible to cormt each separately
owned parcel as one "property owner" for purposes of implementing the "public
generally'' exception. If the city cannot merely "count" parcels, the city would have to
' You have identified parcels within 500 feet of the Downtown District. Please note that while it
may be reasonable to assume that all property owners within a defined geographical area will be affected in
substantially the same manner by a particular decision, typically distance alone does not determine whether
an effect will be substantially similar in marurer. (Derleth Advice Letter, No. A-03-148.)
I
2
3
4.


File No. I-04-076
Page No. 3
review the ownership records for each individual parcel at a given point in time to
ascertain the names of the owner or owners of the parcel and believes this would be
burdensome.
ANALYSIS
Your roquest for informal assistance pertains to the "public generally'' exception
to the conflict-of-interest analysis (Step 7) as applied to an official's interest in real
property. Under this exception, even if a public official determines that his or her
economic interest will experience a material financial effect as a result of a particular
decision, he or she may still participate if the "public generall/'excqrtion applies to the
economic interest triggering the conflict of interest. (Regulation l87O7OX4).)
Regulation 18707 -l provides the requirements for the general exception:
'(a) Except as provided in Govemment Code s*tions
87102.6 and 87103.5, thematerial financial effect of a
govenmental decision on apublic official's economic
interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if both subdivisions (bxl) and (bX2) of this
regulation apply-
O) Significant Segments and Indistinguishable Effects.
(1) Significant Segme,nt. The governmental decision will
affect a 'significant segment'ofthepublic gemerallyif any
of the following are affected as set forth below:
lt...7
(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect apublic
official's real property interest, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of all pnoperty owners or all
homeowners in thejurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners orhomeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency-
fl...7
(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The governmental
decision will affect a public official's economic interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect the
significant segment identified in subdivision (b)(l) ofthis
regulation."
Subdivisions (b)(1)(B) and (b)(2) of this regulation are relevant to your questions
relating to real property.


File No. I-04-076
Page No. 4
Meaning of "Property Owner"
Neither the Act nor the Commission's regulations specifically define the term
"property owner." However, we have previously advised as follows concerning the
definitions of "property owne,f' and "homeownet'':
"Since these terms are not defined in the regulation, we
must assurne that the Commission intemded for the terms to
have their plain meaning. Therefore, a'propefi ownetr'
gen€rallyrefers to aperson who owns improved or
real property (commercial, governmental or
residemtial)- A 'homeowner' appears to mean an individual
who owns residential property that is his or her domicile or
principal place ofresidence. Thus, 'homeovmer' excludes
a person who owns a non-owner occrryied residential
dwelling or commercial structure-" (Furth Advice Letter,
No. A-99-035; Nerland Advice Irtter, No- I-02-059.)
Thereforg each person who owns property as a "property oum€d' is oounted. A
"person" means an individual, proprietorship, firn, partnership, joint ventme, syndicate,
business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, associatiorl committee,
and any other organization or group of persons acting in concert. (Section 52M7.)
Because owning two parcels, for examplg does not make one person become two
persons, the Commission only counts as one property otmer a p€rson who owns multiple
properties. Note that, forpurposes of applying the "public ge,nerally'' exceptiorl if two
individuals jointly own a home that is their domicile orprincipal placeof residence, they
count as two "homeowners." (Furth, supra; Nerland, supra) Also, if two persons
(whether individuals or entities) own one parcel of propert5r, the two persons would count
as two "property ownetrs-"
Pro-rata Ownership
Section 82033 defines an "interest in real property''forpu4rcses of the Act's
obligations imposed on public officials (or other filers) and provides that:
"'Int€rest in real property' includes any leasehold
beneficial or ovr'nership interest or an optior to acquire
such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction
owned directly indirectly or beneficially by the ptblic
fficial, or otherfiler, or his or her immediate family if the
fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollms
($2,000) or more. Interests in real property of an indiyidual
includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,


File No. I-04-076
Page No. 5
a lO-percent interest or greater." (Section 82033.
Emphasis added.)
Please note that this section pertains to the real property held by an official or
other filer and not to property held by otherpersons. Consequently, section 82033 may
provide guidance for purposes of regulation 18707.1, but the section does not control the
interpretation of "property owner" as usd in the regulation. Because neither the Act nor
the Commission's regulations contain a"l0Yo ruIe," or a $2,00O threshold rule (see
section 871030)), forthe "public generall/'exception, anyperson who owns real
property is a "property ownet''rmder regulation 18707.1, regardless of the person's
ownership percentage.3
Real Property Held in a Trust
Since there are no "percentage of ownership" rquirernents under regulation
18707.1, a peruon who possesses an ownership interest in a trust owning one or more
parcels can be counted as a "propertlr olvner."
Counting Parcels
You have asked whether the City of Berricia may cormt parcels ofproperty, rather
than property owners, to deterrnine wheth€,r the "public generally''exception applies.
The exception as provided in regulation l87W.l requires that suMivision (b[l[B) be
met for real property interests- SuMivision (bXlXB) requires that the decision affect
either: ten percent or more of all 'lroperty owners" or all 'ttomeownetrs" in the official's
jurisdiction or district, or 5,(D0 property owners or homeowners in the official's
jurisdiction. The subdivision does not permit an alternate rule based on the number of
parcels affected by the decision. Rather subdivision (bXlXB) refers to the number of
"p€trsons" affected by the decision, as discussed above. This is because this 'lublic
generally'' exception is meant to address the situation where a docision materially affects
an official who owns real property but also materially affects a significant number of
other property o\Mners (persons) too. The nurnber of parcels affected is not determinative
as to the number of property owners affected by a decision since one property owner can
own two, twenty parcels, or 100 parcels, for exarrple. Therefore, the parcels cannot be
counted in lieu ofproperty owners-
You have menfioned the DietrickAdvice Letter, No. I-02-325. With regard to a
particular official, this letter states:
"According to the facts you provided, there are 4,658 lots
in the jurisdiction and 594 will be affected by the decision.
Therefore, roughly 13% of the property owners in the
jurisdiction would be affected by the decision. Since this
number is greater than the required l0% ofproperty owners
' This conclusion is consistent with both the Furth and, Nerland letters. (5ee Furth, supra;
Nerland, supra.)


File No. I-04-A76
Page No. 6
in the jurisdiction, the decision also affects a significant
segment of the real property owners in the jurisdiction. - -."
This letter's conclusion that the decision affected a "significant segment" was
based on the fact assumed that the decision affected l0% ofproperty owners in the
jurisdiction. If the decision, in fact, would not have affected 10% ofproperty owners, the
exception would not apply. As noted in the letter:
"The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its
advice-givingcapacity (InreOglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops.
7l), and our advice is applicable onlyto the extent that
facts provided to us are correct and that all ofthe material
facts have been disclosed."
As a result, whether 10% of the property ownerc are affected is alwa-ys a factual
determination to be made bythe official. Thereforg if an official wishes to useparcel
data to determine whether the "public generallf excqrtion applies, he or she must assess
whether the property ownership in his or her jurisdiction is such that l(PZ ofparcels
affected equates to loo/o or more of all property owners or all homeorryners affected in the
official's jurisdiction or district, or 5,000 property ovrners orhomeowners affected in the
official's jurisdiction. The official may not merely "@rmt parcels" instead ofproperty
owners (i.e., persons) to determine whether the exception applies. However, the official
is free to use any reasonable and objective method to daemtine whether a significant
segment is affected by the decision so long as the method is consistent with the
applicable regulatory provisions. (See discussion below.) For examplg to be consistent
with the regulation, an official must count 'letrsons" rather than merely count parcels, as
discussed above, when applylng the exception for a conflict of interest arising from an
offi cial's real property.
Sources of Informatron
With regmd to determining the financial effect of a decision, we have advised
that, because the Commission is not a finder of facg the official must make a good faith
effort to assess the effect of the decision by using some reasonable and objective method
of valuation. (Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-150.) Such a good faith effort may
require that additional information regardingthepotential financial effect of the decision
be gathered; a public official is not required to but may choose to utilize professional
services, such as those ofa professional appraisal, to assist in assessing the financial
effect of a decision for conflict-of-interest purposes. (I1o Advice Letter, No. A-00".241-)
This same standard of good faith applies to an official's analysis of whether the
financial effect of the decision affects a "significant segment" ofthe public in
"substantially the same miulner" under the "public generally'' exception. Specifically, the
official must make a good faith effort to assess whether the "public generally''exception
applies using some reasonable and objective method consistent with the applicable
regulatory provisions. The Commission's regulations do not impose procedural


File No. I-04-076
Page No. 7
requirements mandating a particular method of data-gathoirg. The official ma5 if he or
she wishes, review ownership records as you have described provided the method meets
this good faith standard.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
dtl'"t
hd
By
I
Bocanegra
Connsel, l*gal Division
NBje

such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction

($2,000) or more. Interests in real property of an indiyidual
includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any

immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a lO-percent interest or greater." (Section 82033.
